A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Chinese, Buddhist, and Japanese traditions · classical antiquity – early modernity

East Asian

East Asian traditions ask what mind is through self-cultivation, moral psychology, emptiness, Buddha-nature, practice, and the patterned relation between heart-mind and world. Confucian, Daoist, Buddhist, and Neo-Confucian thinkers extend the archive beyond the Mediterranean and South Asian lines already on the site.

551 – 479 BCE

Confucius

Cultivation, ritual form, and the moral shaping of heart-mind.

1200 – 1253

Dōgen

Practice-realization, nondual awareness, and the lived discipline of mind.

643 – 712

Fazang

Huayan interpenetration, Buddha-nature, and a relational metaphysics of mind.

632 – 682

Kuiji

Faxiang Yogācāra and the taxonomy of consciousness.

traditionally 6th c. BCE

Laozi

Dao, non-coercive action, and the empty heart-mind attuned to natural process.

4th c. BCE

Mencius

Heart-mind, moral sprouts, and the affective beginnings of virtue.

c. 470 – c. 391 BCE

Mozi

Impartial concern, practical cognition, and disciplined standards of judgment.

1472 – 1529

Wang Yangming

Innate knowing and the unity of knowledge and action.

c. 602 – 664

Xuanzang

Yogācāra consciousness-only and the layered structure of mind.

3rd c. BCE

Xunzi

Ritual training, effortful transformation, and the crafted mind.

538 – 597

Zhiyi

Tiantai meditation, attention, and the integration of doctrine and practice.

1130 – 1200

Zhu Xi

Principle, vital force, and self-cultivation through investigation.

4th c. BCE

Zhuangzi

Transformative perspective, skill, and wandering beyond rigid distinctions.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
